verbatim_text: And so here, I want to do two things. I want to first understand what scientific research problems are actually modelable using some kind of scaling law. That's one thing. And two, I want to also develop scaling laws that take into account many more variables than just pre-training data set size and model size and amount of reasoning and so on. This could potentially take into account every single decision that goes into creating a foundation model, and I want to understand how those variables relate to these downstream scientific tasks. And for that, of course, we're going to need very deep integration with domain scientists to actually understand these problems and how to do this. And with that, this brings us to the end of my talk. Thank you so much, and I'll take any questions. Yes. I want to know, especially to Jenny, I imagine you've already been working with some of these domain experts in physics and other science or whatever. What is really the relationship between reasoning and actual natural science research? Because for a lot of, which I really appreciate, a lot of the talk focused a lot on scaling for reasoning models or skills. And I don't even really know if having physics skills is really a thing, or if having skills necessarily means you're able to find physics discoveries better. So it really is the relationship.
